query: clinical definitions of stubborn behavior in children

retrieved_docs.title: Pennsylvania Child Abuse Recognition and Reporting -- Introduction -- Categories of Child Abuse
retrieved_docs.content: Engaging in any of the following recent "per se" acts Kicking, biting, throwing, burning, stabbing, or cutting a child in a manner that endangers the child Unreasonably restraining or confining a child, based on consideration of the method, location, or duration of the restraint or confinement Forcefully shaking a child under 1 year of age Forcefully slapping or otherwise striking a child under 1 year of age Interfering with the breathing of a child Causing a child to be present at a location while a violation of 18 Pa.C.S. § 7508.2 (relating to the operation of a methamphetamine laboratory) is occurring, provided the violation is being investigated by law enforcement Leaving a child unsupervised with an individual other than the child’s parent, who the actor knows or reasonably should have known: Is required to register as a Tier II or Tier III sexual offender under 42 Pa. C.S. Ch. 97 Subch. H (relating to registration of sexual offenders), where the victim of the sexual offense was under 18 years of age when the crime was committed Has been determined to be a sexually violent predator under 42 Pa. C.S. §9799.24 (relating to assessments) or any of its predecessors Has been determined to be a sexually violent delinquent child as defined in 42 Pa.C.S. § 9799.12 (relating to definitions) Has been determined to be a sexually violent predator under 42 Pa.C.S. § 9799.58 (relating to  assessments) or has to register for life under 42 Pa.C.S. § 9799.55(b) (relating to registration)

retrieved_docs.title: Cognitive Development -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The concept of childhood is relatively new; in most medieval societies, childhood did not exist. At approximately seven years of age, children were considered little adults with similar expectations for a job, marriage, and legal consequences. Charles Darwin originated ideas of childhood development in his work on the origins of ethology (the scientific study of the evolutionary basis of behavior) and "A Biographical Sketch of an Infant," first published in 1877.

retrieved_docs.title: Bayley Scales Of Infant and Toddler Development -- Definition/Introduction -- Difference between BSID III versus BSID 4
retrieved_docs.content: BSID 4 has questions for the caregiver.

retrieved_docs.title: Bayley Scales Of Infant and Toddler Development -- Definition/Introduction -- Target Age Group
retrieved_docs.content: BSID III and BSID -4 are used for the age group starting from 16 days to 42 months.

retrieved_docs.title: Bayley Scales Of Infant and Toddler Development -- Definition/Introduction -- Difference between BSID III versus BSID 4
retrieved_docs.content: Scoring is Dichotomous (1, 0) in BSID III, whereas scoring is polytomous (2,1,0) in BSID4.

retrieved_docs.title: Cognitive Development -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The first stage, Sensorimotor (ages 0 to 2 years of age), is the time when children master two phenomena: causality and object permanence. Infants and toddlers use their sense and motor abilities to manipulate their surroundings and learn about the environment. They understand a cause-and-effect relationship, like shaking a rattle may produce sound and may repeat it or how crying can make the parent(s) rush to give them attention. As the frontal lobe matures and memory develops, children in this age group can imagine what may happen without physically causing an effect; this is the emergence of thought and allows for the planning of actions. Object permanence emerges around six months of age. It is the concept that objects continue to exist even when they are not presently visible.

retrieved_docs.title: Cognitive Development -- Definition/Introduction -- Stages of Cognitive Development (Problem-Solving and Intelligence)
retrieved_docs.content: Children in the preschool stage have a poor concept of cause and may think sickness is due to misbehavior. They are egocentric in their approach and may look at situations from only their point of view, offering comfort from a favorite stuffed toy to an upset loved one. At 36 months, a child can understand simple time concepts, identify shapes, compare two items, and count to three. Play becomes more comprehensive. At 48 months, children can count to four, identify four colors and understand opposites.

retrieved_docs.title: Emergency Department Triage -- Definition/Introduction -- The Australasian Triage Scale
retrieved_docs.content: In specific populations or presentations, special considerations are taken. In pediatric cases, generally, the same standard triage categorization is applied. However, individual department policies may differ, due to some departments offering fast track options for certain populations such as pediatrics or trauma patients. Both of these populations are triaged mostly due to objective clinical urgency. In the case of behavioral patients, both physical and behavioral assessments are used to determine severity. When both physical and behavioral problems are present, the patient is placed in the highest appropriate category.

retrieved_docs.title: Bayley Scales Of Infant and Toddler Development -- Definition/Introduction -- Infant and Childhood Development
retrieved_docs.content: Infant and childhood development comprises physical, cognitive, social-emotional, linguistic, and behavioral milestones. The differences in cultural, environmental, and genetic factors influence development. The development process is rapid and cumulative. Developmental disabilities are common and are reported in 1 in 6 children in the United States. The number of children with select developmental disabilities (autism, attention deficit hyperactivity disorder, and other developmental delays) has increased, requiring more health and education services. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Bayley Scales Of Infant and Toddler Development -- Definition/Introduction -- Difference between BSID III versus BSID 4
retrieved_docs.content: Compared to BSID III, BSID 4 takes approximately 30% less time to complete the assessment.

retrieved_docs.title: Bayley Scales Of Infant and Toddler Development -- Definition/Introduction -- Purpose
retrieved_docs.content: The BSID determines children's developmental functioning and plans management for kids with developmental delays. BSID helps in the following ways. Early identification of intellectual delay. Re-assessments can help to monitor progress over time. To individualize the management and to accommodate a child's developmental and learning needs. To assess individual domains of development, such as cognitive delay, To help researchers as a research tool. Pediatric professionals are critical in diagnosing developmental disabilities early. [6] BSID helps to detect developmental delay early and also to initiate early developmental intervention.

retrieved_docs.title: Bayley Scales Of Infant and Toddler Development -- Definition/Introduction -- Preparation
retrieved_docs.content: The examiner explains to the caretaker what happens during the assessment. This helps establish rapport with the child and avoid parental concerns during the evaluation. The caretaker is advised not to talk or aid the child during the assessment, thereby avoiding skewed deviation.

retrieved_docs.title: Bayley Scales Of Infant and Toddler Development -- Definition/Introduction -- BSID Kit
retrieved_docs.content: Each BSID kit has a manual, forms, a booklet for the motor response, Social-Emotional, Adaptive Behavior domain questionnaires, caregiver reports, an observation Checklist, and a manipulative set. Cognitive, Language, and Motor scores assessment needs a web-based device. The questionnaire for caregivers includes socio-emotional and adaptive domains. Scores are given as polytomous, ranging from 0 to 2. In BSID-4 scoring, 2 are Mastery, 1 is emerging, and 0 means absent. After the assessment, the assessor summarizes standard and scaled scores. BSID administration requires training, which happens through webinars and workshops.

retrieved_docs.title: Bayley Scales Of Infant and Toddler Development -- Definition/Introduction -- Difference between BSID III versus BSID 4
retrieved_docs.content: Scoring is software-based in BSID III. BSID 4 scores need web-based administration.

retrieved_docs.title: Bayley Scales Of Infant and Toddler Development -- Definition/Introduction -- Difference between BSID III versus BSID 4
retrieved_docs.content: BSID 4 retains the same 5 domains as BSIDIII. However, the number of items in each section has decreased. BSID III has the following number of items in each domain Cognitive scale - 91 items Language scale - 49 items in the receptive and 48 items in the expressive domain Motor scale - 66 items in the fine motor and 72 items in the gross motor domain Social-Emotional Scale derived from Greenspan Chart. Adaptive behavior scale derived from ABAS (Adaptive behavior assessment system) BSID4 has the following number of items Cognitive scale - 81 items Language scale - 42 items in the receptive and 37 items in the expressive domain Motor scale - 46 items in the fine motor and 58 items in the gross motor domain Social-Emotional Scale is unmodified from Bayley III. The adaptive behavior scale utilizes the Vineland behavior assessment system.

retrieved_docs.title: Bayley Scales Of Infant and Toddler Development -- Definition/Introduction -- Developmental Assessments
retrieved_docs.content: An estimated 5 to 10% of the pediatric population has a developmental disability. [2] There are 3 different strategies to detect developmental delay. Routine developmental surveillance is where the health care professional takes a short history and briefly examines it to elicit any developmental delay. Identification of developmental delay is ineffective when based solely on routine surveillance. [2] A developmental screening tool is a systematic and structured tool to identify developmental delay. Although screening tools are cost and time-efficient, they are not diagnostic and have less utility in high-risk populations. [3] Formal developmental tests are widely accepted and have a diagnostic utility.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1826
retrieved_docs.content: The category “signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions” is presented as a parent concept that includes the specific child entity “غشي.” In this framework, the general term “Symptoms” is described as closely related to, and potentially synonymous with, the broader phrase “signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions,” indicating that the two expressions refer to overlapping clinical manifestations. Consequently, the specific manifestation “غشي” is understood to fall under the umbrella of nonspecific or ill‑defined symptomatology, and clinicians should recognize its inclusion within this broader symptom category when evaluating and documenting patient presentations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1771
retrieved_docs.content: Symptoms are closely related to and may be considered synonymous with the broader category of signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions. In this hierarchical framework, diseases and injuries are classified as having the parent category of signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions, indicating that they are understood through the manifestation of such nonspecific signs. A specific example of a child element within this category is الانهيار في الدورة الدموية (circulatory collapse), which represents a distinct clinical manifestation that can arise from ill‑defined conditions. Together, these relationships highlight that the identification of symptoms and signs, including serious events like circulatory collapse, is essential for recognizing and managing diseases and injuries that present without clear, well‑defined diagnostic criteria.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2320
retrieved_docs.content: The gestational period of 29‑30 weeks completed is directly linked to the outcome of a liveborn infant, indicating that reaching this stage of pregnancy can result in a newborn who is alive at birth. In the broader classification of pregnancy timelines, the concept of Duration of gestation encompasses liveborn as a specific category or outcome, reflecting that livebirth is considered one of the possible results within the overall spectrum of gestational durations. This relationship underscores the clinical relevance of gestational age in determining the likelihood of a live birth.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 4 -- Community 2861
retrieved_docs.content: The Pediatric Adverse Events Terminology encompasses a range of specific adverse‑event concepts relevant to children, and it includes the concept تدلّي الحبل السري (umbilical cord prolapse). By incorporating this particular adverse event, the terminology provides clinicians and researchers with a standardized term for identifying, reporting, and managing umbilical cord prolapse in pediatric patients, thereby supporting consistent communication and improved patient safety.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 102
retrieved_docs.content: The concept of a liveborn infant encompasses a range of perinatal classifications that are linked through gestational age, birthweight, and fetal growth status. A gestational age of 29‑30 weeks completed is identified as a specific child category of liveborn, indicating that infants born alive at this stage are included within the broader liveborn group. The duration of gestation has an inverse‑isa relationship with liveborn, reflecting that shorter gestational periods are associated with distinct clinical classifications of liveborn infants. One major classification of liveborn infants is short gestation; low birth weight; and fetal growth retardation. This category both classifies liveborn and is self‑referential, emphasizing its central role in describing infants born with reduced gestational length, low birth weight, or impaired growth. The disorder relating to short gestation AND/OR low birthweight is positioned as a parent of liveborn, indicating that this disorder underlies the

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 1983
retrieved_docs.content: The concept of “Duration of gestation” is classified within the International Statistical Classification of Diseases and Related Health Problems, 10th Revision, Australian Modification (ICD‑10‑AM), indicating that gestational length is a recognized medical category in this coding system. Within this framework, the specific gestational interval of “29‑30 weeks of gestation completed” is linked to the outcome of a “liveborn” infant, reflecting that infants born after completing 29 to 30 weeks of pregnancy can be alive at birth. Accordingly, the “liveborn” outcome is treated as a distinct subclass or child concept under the broader umbrella of gestational duration, illustrating how gestational age ranges and birth outcomes are organized together in clinical classification and patient‑care documentation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 1886
retrieved_docs.content: Behavioral/psychiatric manifestations are classified as a subcategory of the broader entity الهَذَيان, indicating that these mental and behavioral signs are considered part of the larger domain represented by الهَذَيان. This hierarchical relationship helps organize clinical features, allowing clinicians to recognize that behavioral and psychiatric presentations fall within the overarching concept of الهَذَيان and to consider them in the context of that broader classification when assessing and managing patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 1 -- Community 232
retrieved_docs.content: with attention to contraindications, and how structured terminologies provide organized frameworks for perinatal and pediatric diagnoses, supporting comprehensive patient assessment and care planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 1 -- Community 202
retrieved_docs.content: The medical framework links gestational age, birth outcomes, and perinatal disorders through a hierarchical classification system. A gestational period of 29‑30 weeks completed is identified as a precursor to a liveborn outcome, indicating that infants born after this duration can survive outside the womb. Duration of gestation is positioned as a broader category that inversely encompasses liveborn, underscoring that the length of pregnancy defines the viability of a live birth. Conditions associated with short gestation and low birth weight — collectively described as Short gestation; low birth weight; and fetal growth retardation — serve as a primary classifier for liveborn infants, reflecting the clinical importance of prematurity and insufficient fetal growth in determining neonatal status. This same condition is also classified as ولادة بأقصى درجات الابتسار، غير محددة {الوزن} (“birth at the highest degree of prematurity, unspecified weight”), reinforcing its role as a marker of

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 1627
retrieved_docs.content: General injuries represent a category of traumatic abnormality that serves as a parent concept for more specific injury types. Within this broader classification, the narrower subcategories include chest injury (إصابة الصدر) and skin injury (إصابة في الجلد). By organizing these specific injuries as child entities of general injuries, clinicians can more precisely identify, document, and manage the particular anatomical locations and characteristics of traumatic events.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 2 -- Community 998
retrieved_docs.content: Malaise and fatigue, which is mapped to the Arabic expressions الشعور بالضعف and النوام and also associated with الضعف العصبي, serves as a parent category for the broader grouping termed signs and symptoms of ill-defined conditions. Within this grouping, the concept غشي is identified as a child element. Additionally, the term Symptoms is described as related and possibly synonymous with signs and symptoms of ill-defined conditions, indicating overlap in clinical description. Understanding these hierarchical and synonymous relationships helps clinicians recognize that malaise and fatigue and its Arabic equivalents encompass a range of nonspecific signs, including غشي, and that the label Symptoms may be used interchangeably with the broader category of signs and symptoms of ill-defined conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 1575
retrieved_docs.content: Newborn Screening Terminology encompasses a specific subset of concepts that includes تسمم الحمل, قَيْء الحمل خفيف الوطأة, and plod. These entities represent distinct conditions or terms that are incorporated within the standardized language used for newborn screening, facilitating precise identification, classification, and communication of relevant neonatal health issues.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 1246
retrieved_docs.content: الذهان (الهَذَيان) is a broad psychiatric disorder that serves as the parent category for various Behavioral/psychiatric manifestations, indicating that these manifestations are subcomponents of the psychotic spectrum. Within this overarching concept, الذهَان also includes specific conditions such as حالات ذُهانية عضوية عابرة, which are transient organic psychotic states. Recognizing that Behavioral/psychiatric manifestations fall under الذهَان and that transient organic psychoses are a child concept of الذهَان helps clinicians appreciate the full range of psychotic presentations and informs comprehensive assessment and management strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2353
retrieved_docs.content: Observation and evaluation of newborns for suspected condition not found is a specific form of medical examination/evaluation; it falls under the broader category of medical examinations and evaluations performed to assess health status. This particular assessment focuses on newborns when a suspected condition cannot be identified, ensuring that clinicians systematically observe and evaluate the infant to confirm the absence of disease or to detect subtle signs that may require further investigation. By classifying this newborn assessment as a subset of general medical examination/evaluation, healthcare providers recognize its role within routine pediatric care and its importance for early detection, reassurance, and appropriate follow‑up when a condition is not initially found.

Together, these relationships delineate a structured view of how adverse reactions to antidepressants, opioid and cocaine withdrawal, SSRI withdrawal, and related physiological rebound effects are interrelated within the taxonomy of psychoactive substance‑induced disorders, providing clinicians with a comprehensive framework for diagnosis, causative agent identification, and management of withdrawal‑related conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2850
retrieved_docs.content: Pediatric Adverse Events Terminology incorporates the concept وفاة حديث الولادة, which denotes neonatal death, as a specific entry within its classification of pediatric adverse events. By including neonatal death in this terminology, healthcare providers can standardize reporting, track incidence, and implement targeted strategies to improve safety and outcomes for newborn patients.
